legacy.full_text:
Reasons I will not own a Tesla Truck, or any fully electric vehicles. EVER....

- Remote dependency: A truck that needs a server to act normal isn’t a truck, it’s a leash with tires.

- App control: When your phone is the key, the cloud is the master.

result.text:
Reasons I will not own a Tesla Truck, or any fully electric vehicles. EVER....

- Remote dependency: A truck that needs a server to act normal isn’t a truck, it’s a leash with tires.

- App control: When your phone is the key, the cloud is the master.

- Remote lock/start: Convenience is the bait; control is the hook.

- Location tracking: Your vehicle shouldn’t report coordinates like a parole monitor with cupholders.

- Software features: If an app controls windows, climate, tailgate, charging, lights, and ride height, you rent permissions.

- Speed Limit Mode: Once a vehicle can be software-limited, the question becomes: who gets the button next?

- OTA updates: You bought one truck, but tomorrow the mothership can install a different one.

- Feature paywalls: Hardware you paid for should never beg a corporation for permission to function.

- Subscription creep: They sell you the truck once, then sell you pieces of it forever like digital crack dealers.

- Account lockout: Lose the account, lose the kingdom, cloud feudalism on wheels.

- Telemetry: A real truck hauls weight; this thing hauls your data back to the plantation.

- Driving tracking: Hard braking, speed, routes, habits,  your vehicle becomes a stainless steel snitch.

- Insurance weaponization: The car rats, the insurer smiles, and your premium gets Skull-Fucked.

- Government access: Once the data exists, the subpoena machine starts licking its chops.

- Sensitive location exposure: Your truck should not know enough to build a prosecution map.

- Hackable surface: Cellular, Bluetooth, Wi-Fi, APIs, apps, cloud, every “smart” feature is another door.

- Brick-by-bug: Mechanical trucks break from worn parts; software trucks break because an update sneezed wrong.

- Cloud outage risk: If the network dies and your truck gets stupid, it was never sovereign.

- Right-to-repair squeeze: You own the metal, but they own the spell required to fix it.

- Service lock-in: When only the manufacturer can bless the repair, you’re not a customer, YOU ARE livestock.

- Parts pairing: A bolt-on part needing software approval is tyranny wearing a torque wrench.

- Resale friction: Selling a truck should require title and keys, not sacrifice to cloud gods.

- Digital transfer: When ownership needs server approval, property rights get kneecapped.

- Policy risk: Terms of service should never touch your steering wheel.

- Acceptable-use creep: Today it’s app rules; tomorrow it’s “your mobility privileges are under review.”

- Behavior nudging: They don’t have to force you when software can herd you like cattle.

- Navigation influence: The map isn’t neutral when the company controls the rails.

- Charging dependence: If mobility depends on approved networks, freedom has a plug in its neck.

- Dead-zone weakness: A real machine gets mean off-grid; a cloud truck gets confused.

- Remote diagnostics: Diagnostics are fine, until “checking” becomes “controlling.”

- Remote service commands: Anything powerful enough to fix you remotely can restrict you remotely.

- Recall-by-software: They fix bugs over the air,  and rewrite ownership without touching the driveway.

- Feature removal: If a feature can be activated remotely, it can be revoked remotely. Rental cosplay.

- Privacy decay: The more connected the truck gets, the more your private life becomes a corporate spreadsheet.

- Centralized control: One company, one account, one cloud, one kill-switch-shaped architecture. What could go wrong?

- Precedent risk: The danger isn’t what they admit today,  it’s what the architecture allows tomorrow.

- False toughness: Stainless steel doesn’t make it sovereign. A prison cell can be stainless too.

- Dystopian core: It looks like Mad Max, but operates like a locked iPad.

The Cybertruck is sold as apocalypse armor, but built like a rolling terms-of-service agreement.  

NO TY. FUUUUUUUCK ALL THAT. 🤣

Just did a Cybertruck demo and… wow… I get it now…

It was my first time ever driving a Tesla and first time using FSD… It’s absolutely insane. I knew it would be good but didn’t realize it was THAT good… 

If you want to see the most pure and joyful reactions to FSD, here’s https://t.co/iO1813dB2C

result.text:
Just did a Cybertruck demo and… wow… I get it now…

It was my first time ever driving a Tesla and first time using FSD… It’s absolutely insane. I knew it would be good but didn’t realize it was THAT good… 

If you want to see the most pure and joyful reactions to FSD, here’s my pregnant wife and 2.5 year old son experiencing it for the first time…

We returned the demo and ordered a Cybertruck two hours later…

Elon Musk deserves to be a trillionaire and anyone who says differently should be scorned then ignored.

legacy.full_text:
The people that claim these things have no idea that Tesla stores all the telemetry and data before the crash.

You can access steering angles, accelerator/brake pedal positions, etc. It’s VERY easy for Tesla to prove it was human error.

We have heard many such claims in the past. It always turns out to be the human drivers fault. 

I suspect this will be no different. FSD does not drive into brick walls. https://t.co/lwmiqlQS4x
Tesla Semi cannot scale fast enough
